facade_colour: bright yellow-painted brick with red trim and white arched window surrounds

hcd_data.architectural_style: Converted House-form, Row, Bay-and-Gable (confirmed – HCD Plan Vol. 2)
hcd_data.statement_of_contribution: Each property has value as a nineteenth-century House-form building that has been modified to accommodate a commercial conversion through a one-storey commercial addition. Through its construction date and typology, each property is associated with the District’s early residential development from the 1850s to the early 1900s. With its visibly layered built form, each property is also associated with the patterns of commercial adaptation and expansion that began in the early twentieth century. Constructed as a row, the properties have evolved over time while retaining original building features reflecting vernacular interpretations of Victorian architectural styles, including their brick cladding and window openings above the first storey, and cross-gabled roofline. Each property has contextual value because it supports and maintains the historic mixed use character of the area and is physically, functionally, visually, and historically linked to the surrounding market area.

context.street_character: One-way WB | 30 km/h | Sub-standard sidewalks & roadway | E-W commercial connector

photo_observations.facade_colour_observed: bright yellow-painted brick with red trim and white arched window surrounds
photo_observations.facade_condition_notes: Facade is brightly repainted and active; decorative trim is weathered but the building reads as occupied and maintained.
photo_observations.window_details: Large display window and entry at grade, two upper-storey windows with arched white surrounds, and one small gable/attic opening visible.
photo_observations.door_details: Single glazed commercial door recessed slightly behind the sign band.
photo_observations.cornice_details: Simple projecting sign band below the eaves.
photo_observations.chimney_details: No chimney clearly visible on the front-facing view.
photo_observations.decorative_details: Arched white-painted surrounds emphasize the upper windows within the house-form facade.
photo_observations.storefront_description: Cheese shop storefront inserted into a brightly painted house-form commercial building.
photo_observations.signage_observed: Cheese Magic signs on the large yellow fascia and lower panels.
photo_observations.alterations_visible: Bright repainting and commercial signage heavily modify the original domestic-looking facade.
photo_observations.notes: The building retains a clear house-form profile despite the storefront conversion.

storefront_description: Cheese shop storefront inserted into a brightly painted house-form commercial building.
notes: The building retains a clear house-form profile despite the storefront conversion.

facade_detail.composition: The street elevation spans roughly 5.2 metres across 3 storeys and is capped by a cross-gable, consistent with its converted house-form, row, bay-and-gable typology.
facade_detail.opening_rhythm: The facade keeps a domestic opening rhythm of 1, 2, 1 window bays from lower to upper levels, using mixed, and 1 principal entrance.
facade_detail.heritage_expression: Character-defining facade elements include decorative brick, original windows, cornice.
facade_detail.heritage_summary: Constructed as a row, the properties have evolved over time while retaining original building features reflecting vernacular interpretations of Victorian architectural styles, including their brick cladding and window openings above the first storey, and cross-gabled roofline. With its visibly layered built form, each property is also associated with the patterns of commercial adaptation and expansion that began in the early twentieth century.

facade_description: Three-storey converted row house with a steep front-gabled roof, brightly repainted in yellow with red trim. The upper floors have a symmetric 1-2-1 window arrangement; the second-storey windows feature white arched surrounds that are the most prominent decorative element on the facade. A Cheese Magic storefront occupies the ground floor behind a simple projecting sign band. The house-form profile remains clearly legible despite the bold commercial repainting.
heritage_summary: Constructed as a row, the properties have evolved over time while retaining original building features reflecting vernacular interpretations of Victorian architectural styles, including their brick cladding and window openings above the first storey, and cross-gabled roofline. With its visibly layered built form, each property is also associated with the patterns of commercial adaptation and expansion that began in the early twentieth century.
